scenario_title: TTL mismatch among RRset members added by UPDATE
test_rationale: This test starts with a simple A RRset for host5.campus.edu. and issues one syntactically valid UPDATE that adds two more A records for the same owner using conflicting TTLs. That directly exercises the RFC 2136 gap around how an UPDATE processor enforces RFC 2181’s equal-TTL RRset invariant. The follow-up QUERY makes the outcome binary by revealing whether the server accepted and canonicalized the RRset or rejected the UPDATE and preserved the original state.

group_1.behavior: Accepts the UPDATE and canonicalizes the RRset to a single TTL, typically preserving the existing TTL or normalizing the set before serving.
group_1.post_state: host5.campus.edu. has an A RRset with both added addresses present, served with one TTL value.

group_2.behavior: Rejects the UPDATE because the input attempts to create an unequal-TTL RRset, leaving the original RRset unchanged.
group_2.post_state: host5.campus.edu. remains only the original A RRset with 192.0.2.90 TTL 300.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2181 §5.2 requires all RRs in an RRset to have the same TTL, but RFC 2136 §3.4.2 does not specify whether an UPDATE that adds same-owner/same-type RRs with differing TTLs must be rejected, normalized, or accepted and rewritten. Servers therefore diverge both in UPDATE success/failure and in whether the post-update RRset contains the new records.
